1: אֲמָרַי (ʾamāray, "my words") and הֲגִיגִי (hăgîgî, "my groaning") — two distinct levels of speech. ʾImrāh (words, utterances) refers to articulated speech; hăgîg (from hāgāh) is the inarticulate murmuring of Psalms 1:2, the inward meditation that may be pre-verbal. Both are addressed to the LORD: the fully-formed words and the groan that underlies them. The superscription "for the flutes" (הַנְּחִילוֹת, hanĕḥîlôt) is uncertain — possibly meaning "for wind instruments" or referring to a tune.
2: מַלְכִּי וֵאלֹהַי (malkî wēʾlōhay, "my King and my God") — the double vocative places God in the role of sovereign ruler whom the Psalmist approaches as a subject approaching the throne. The prayer is a royal petition, not merely a private devotion. ʾElōhay (my God) with the first-person suffix: personal relationship, not generic deity.
3: בֹּקֶר תִּשְׁמַע קוֹלִי (bōqer tishmaʿ qôlî, "in the morning you hear my voice") — the morning is the time of the daily sacrifice and the moment when petitions were heard. אֲעָרְכָה-לְּךָ (ʾăʿārkāh-llĕkā, "I lay my prayer before you") — ʿārak: to arrange in order, to set in array (as a battle formation or an offering laid out). Prayer as deliberate, ordered presentation — not random petition but careful placement. אֲצַפֶּה (ʾăṣappeh, "and wait") — ṣāpāh: to watch/look out (as a watchman on a wall). Prayer followed by watchful waiting for the answer.
4: לֹא אֵל-חָפֵץ רֶשַׁע אָתָּה (lōʾ ʾêl-ḥāpēṣ reshaʿ ʾāttāh, "you are not a God who takes pleasure in wickedness") — the logic of prayer: the God addressed is one whose character makes wickedness alien. Ḥāpēṣ (to delight in/desire) — not mere neutral toleration but active delight; God has no such delight in reshaʿ (wickedness — the condition of being fundamentally wrong/twisted). יָגֻר (yāgur, "cannot dwell") from gûr: to sojourn, to lodge as a guest. Evil cannot even temporarily reside with God.
5: לֹא-יִתְיַצְּבוּ הוֹלְלִים (lōʾ-yityaṣṣĕbû hôlĕlîm, "the arrogant cannot stand in your sight") — hôlĕlîm (from hālal, root of Hallelujah): here in a negative sense, those who are boastful, bragging fools. They "cannot stand" (yityaṣṣĕbû, hithpael of nāṣab: to take one's stand, to present oneself) — judicial language: they have no standing to appear before this God. שָׂנֵאתָ (śānêʾtā, "you hate") — the perfect of established, settled disposition: not a momentary emotion but a stable orientation of God against evil-workers.
6: תְּאַבֵּד דֹּבְרֵי כָזָב (tĕʾabbêd dōbĕrê kāzāb, "you destroy those who speak falsehood") — ʾābad (piel): to cause to perish, to destroy utterly. The specific evil targeted is speech: false testimony, lies. תְּעֵב יְהוָה (tĕʿêb YHWH, "the LORD abhors") — tāʿab: to abhor, to find detestable (used of the ritual abomination of idols and the moral abomination of injustice). The ʾîsh-dāmîm (man of bloodshed) and the mirmāh (deceitful) man are equally objects of divine abhorrence.
7: בְּרֹב חַסְדְּךָ (bĕrōb ḥasdĕkā, "through the abundance of your steadfast love") — ḥesed (covenant loyalty, steadfast love) is the ground of access. Not merit but ḥesed opens the way in. אֶשְׁתַּחֲוֶה (ʾeshtaḥăweh, "I will bow down") — hithpael of shāḥāh: the full prostration of reverence. The Psalmist who enters by grace bows in reverence — access through ḥesed does not reduce the awe of presence.
8: הוֹרֵנִי יְהוָה דַּרְכֶּךָ (hôrênî YHWH darkkekā, "lead me, O LORD, in your righteousness") — hiphil imperative of yārāh, the same root as tôrāh: "teach me/point me/direct me." The prayer for divine direction uses the word from which Torah gets its meaning. לְמַעַן שׁוֹרְרָי (lĕmaʿan shôrĕrāy, "because of those who lie in wait for me") — shōrĕr is from the root meaning to watch/observe with hostile intent. Those who spy on the Psalmist create the urgency for divine guiding — without it, he will walk into their traps.
9: קִבְרָם גְּרוֹנָם (qibrām gerônām, "their throat is an open grave") — one of the most striking images in the Psalter: the speech coming out of the enemy's throat has the quality of an open tomb — the stench of death and corruption. Qeber (grave) and gārôn (throat/neck) are in a construct chain: their throat is a-grave-of-them, a grave that belongs to and characterizes them. Paul cites this in Romans 3:13 as part of his catena of OT verses demonstrating universal human sinfulness. חֲלַק (ḥālāq, "flatter") — to make smooth; their tongues are smooth/slippery — the polished, dangerous speech of flattery that conceals hostile intent.
10: הַאֲשִׁימֵם אֱלֹהִים (haʾăshîmêm ʾĕlōhîm, "Hold them guilty, O God") — hiphil of ʾāsham: to declare guilty, to cause to bear their own guilt. This is an imprecatory element — the Psalmist calls on God to bring justice on the wicked. The grounds are explicit: "for their many rebellions (פִּשְׁעֵיהֶם, pishʿêhem)" and "for they have defied you (מָרוּ בָךְ)." The judgment called for is not personal revenge but divine justice against covenant-breaking rebellion.
11: יִשְׂמְחוּ כָל-חוֹסֵי בָךְ (yismĕḥû kol-ḥôsê vāk, "let all who take refuge in you rejoice") — ḥāsāh (to take refuge) is one of the key Psalmic words for trust — running to God for shelter as one runs to a fortress. The call to joy (śāmaḥ) and shout (rānan) is grounded in the security of refuge, not the absence of enemies. תָּסֵךְ עָלֵימוֹ (tāsêk ʿālêmô, "spread your protection") — sākak: to cover over, to provide shelter; the canopy of divine protection.
12: כַּצִּנָּה רָצוֹן תַּעְטְרֶנּוּ (kaṣṣinnāh rāṣôn taʿṭĕrennû, "you surround him with favor as with a shield") — ṣinnāh is the large body-shield (contrast māgên of Psalms 3:3) — the shield that covers the whole person. Rāṣôn (favor, goodwill, delight) surrounds the righteous as a shield surrounds a soldier: divine favor is the outer layer protecting the one who trusts. The psalm ends by answering its opening: the one who prays in the morning (v3) is the one whom God shields.
